title: Peace through the Blood of the Covenant
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Peace through the Blood of the Covenant - Hebrews 13_20-25 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.point: I. Who God Is: The God of Peace
subpoints: A. God addresses a church in turmoil considering a return to Temple Judaism
B. Peace in Scripture: not merely the absence of conflict but wholeness and completeness
C. God is the God of the peace—it cannot be found anywhere else

outline.point: II. God Raised Jesus Through the Blood of the Everlasting Covenant
subpoints: A. The resurrection proves that the Father accepted the sacrifice of the Son
B. Jesus entered the Most Holy Place with his own blood, not animals
C. The blood of the covenant is everlasting—nothing more is needed

outline.point: III. God Calls Jesus the Great Shepherd of the Sheep
subpoints: A. Sheep cannot live, find food, or fight predators without a shepherd
B. We are the sheep—the Bible never makes us the hero
C. The tender shepherd gathers lambs and gently leads (Isaiah 40:11)

outline.point: IV. What God Does: Equip Us for Every Good Work
subpoints: A. The word complete: mending a net, setting a broken bone, restoring a brother
B. God equips with every good thing we need through Christ's blood
C. Running from Christ is running from the very means of peace and wholeness

outline.point: V. How We Respond: Obedience and Doxology
subpoints: A. God equips us not for self-actualization but to do his will
B. The correct response is doxology: to him be glory forever and ever
C. Practical: do not forsake church, Bible reading, or prayer—especially when you do not want to

summary:
Preaching the magnificent benediction of Hebrews 13:20-25, Pastor Tito Lyro traces three movements: who God is, what God does, and how we respond. God is 'the God of the peace'—not a general peacefulness but the very wholeness and satisfaction the human heart craves, found only in him through the Lord Jesus Christ. He established this peace by raising Jesus from the dead through the blood of the everlasting covenant, proving the Father received the Son's sacrifice on behalf of his people.

The sermon turns on the beautiful description of Jesus as 'that great shepherd of the sheep,' exploring how helpless sheep are without a shepherd and how tenderly Christ leads, gathers, and provides. Because of who God is and what he has done, his people are equipped with every good thing they need to do his will—through his Word, his Spirit, his undershepherds, and his people. The sermon closes with a doxological application: when we run from the means of grace, we run from peace itself; and when we behold who God is and what he has done, we cannot help but break into praise.
transcript:
## Introduction

This morning I want to consider with you one of the most beautiful benedictory passages in the New Testament. There are several doxological passages in the New Testament, and this one is probably the most beautiful — this great benediction and doxology that we find in verses 20 through 21 of Hebrews 13. It is both a doxology, ascribing glory to Christ, and a benediction, blessing the congregation the original author is writing to and, through them, blessing us through the blood of the everlasting covenant.

As we saw in verses 18 and 19 just before this great passage, the author of the book of Hebrews asked the church to pray for him so that he might be united to them. Now he prays for them. And in this prayer he tells us who God is, what God does, and how we respond to who God is and what he does. Three parts: who God is, what God does, and how we respond to those two truths.

I will tell you right off the bat that the first point is longer than the others, so do not get anxious if it takes a while to get through the first point.

## I. Who God Is: The God of Peace

We start seeing who God is in verse 20. The Holy Spirit says, "Now may the God of peace." He addresses God as *the* God of peace.

The Hebrew church — the church this letter was originally written to — was in turmoil. They were seriously considering putting Jesus aside and going back to Temple Judaism. They were thinking: being a Christian is just about too much trouble. Can we just go back to the way things were before we professed faith in Jesus Christ? Can we go back to Temple sacrifice? Can we go back to the synagogue and worship God the way we did before? They had been kicked out of the synagogue because of their faith in Christ. They were being shunned from the Jewish community. They thought a return to Temple Judaism would bring peace to their lives.

And in that context, the Holy Spirit speaks of God as the God of peace.

But he says this God of peace is a specific God, identified in verse 20:

> *"Now may the God of peace who brought up our Lord Jesus from the dead, that great Shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant..."* (Hebrews 13:20)

The Holy Spirit shows them that God's identity as the God of peace is inseparably tied to the work and resurrection of Christ. There is no way to go back to Temple Judaism, no way to go back to a time before Christ, because God's identity is precisely that he is the one who brought Jesus back from the dead. That is who God is. If they go back to the synagogue, go back to the period before faith in Christ, they may have horizontal peace with those around them — but there will be no peace with God apart from the Lord Jesus Christ.

### What Biblical Peace Means

This word *peace* is important. The biblical concept of peace includes the absence of conflict and turmoil, but it is much closer to our concept of wholeness and completeness than just the absence of conflict. It is a more positive idea than simply having no fights. It is something like having someone present in your life — the Lord Jesus Christ. That is what peace in the Scriptures means: the presence of God, further revealed to us as the presence of the Lord Jesus Christ. Remember that Jesus Christ is prophesied as being Immanuel — God with us. That is what peace is: the presence of Christ in our lives.

And even though it does not show in our English translation, when the author of Hebrews says "may the God of peace," in the original language he actually says *the* God of *the* peace — with a definite article. This is not a general peace, not a general sense of peacefulness, but the very peace, the very completeness, the very wholeness, the satisfaction that a human heart craves. The implication of the definite article is that this peace cannot be found anywhere else. It is the peace we need. It is the peace we crave. It cannot be found anywhere else except in the God of the peace who brought the Lord Jesus back from the dead.

The source of this peace is God. Therefore a relationship with him, and prayer to this God of peace, brings peace. There is no way to have peace apart from the Lord Jesus Christ.

Remember what the Apostle Paul told us in Philippians 4:6-7:

> *"Be anxious for nothing, but in everything by prayer and supplication, with thanksgiving, let your requests be made known to God; and the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus."* (Philippians 4:6-7)

Notice that the result of bringing everything before God — all the things causing you to be anxious — is not necessarily a change in circumstances, but a removal of anxiety. Paul does not say God will remove the things that are causing you anxiety. Rather, through the Prince of Peace, your very anxiety is removed. That happens because at that moment we are finding our wholeness and satisfaction in God, not in the issues of life. As we stand before the God of peace who brought again our shepherd from the dead, we are beholding him, satisfied with him, complete in him — not in the things going on in our lives. And if we are complete in God, and he promises to never leave us or forsake us, then our hearts will be guarded against anxiety.

Brothers and sisters — and any unbelievers in our midst — this peace can only be found through Jesus Christ. The night before his crucifixion, Jesus said to his disciples, and through them to us:

> *"Peace I leave with you, My peace I give to you; not as the world gives do I give to you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid."* (John 14:27)

He is not talking about a general peacefulness. God's peace is man's peace *with* God. That is the ultimate peace. Our God is the God of peace because he established peace with us. In Romans 5:1, the Apostle Paul says that having been justified by faith in Jesus Christ, we are at peace with God. Believer, God is not angry with you. He is not shaking his fist at you, not frowning upon you. He is at peace with you — if you have been justified through the Lord Jesus Christ, because he is the God of peace through the Lord Jesus Christ.

God does not only exemplify peace but also promotes peace and wholeness among his people. Our God is the God of Shalom — the God of completeness and wholeness — as he restores us to himself.

### God Raised Jesus Through the Blood of the Everlasting Covenant

So God is the God of peace, but he is more than that. He is the God who raised our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ from the dead. God is the God of peace because of the Lord Jesus Christ himself. God raised his Son from the dead because of the blood that Jesus shed on behalf of those who believe in him.

This might sound obvious, but consider: the means the Father used to raise the Lord Jesus from the dead was death. What gave Christ the right to be raised from the dead was his death, his shed blood. When he came before the Father offering himself, the Father said, "I receive it on behalf of my people" — and he proved that by raising him from the dead to eternal life. The resurrection is a declaration that what Jesus did *worked*.

Earlier in Hebrews chapter 9, the author writes:

> *"Not with the blood of goats and calves, but with His own blood He entered the Most Holy Place once for all, having obtained eternal redemption."* (Hebrews 9:12)

People of God, on that Friday, Jesus Christ walked as it were through the spiritual tabernacle — this is figurative language, but it gives us the picture — not with a goat, not with a bull, not with the blood of animals, but offering himself. And he obtained your redemption in doing that. God through Jesus Christ is a God of peace, the one who obtained your salvation once for all. Nothing else you have to do. As Toplady says, "Nothing in my hands I bring, simply to the cross I cling."

And notice how the author refers to Jesus in verse 20 — not just as the risen Lord, but as *that great Shepherd of the sheep*, through the blood of the everlasting covenant. The everlasting covenant — the new covenant so eloquently described in chapter 8 — is the forever way that God relates to us through the blood of the Lord Jesus Christ. There is no possibility of it becoming obsolete. There is no possibility of God needing something else besides the blood of Jesus in order to keep you close to him. The blood of Jesus shed on the cross of Calvary, received in glory by the resurrection — that is everlasting, that is eternal. You do not need something else. The blood of Jesus never loses its power, and therefore God is always the God of peace for those who believe.

### Jesus the Great Shepherd

Notice how he identifies Jesus again in verse 20: "that great Shepherd of the sheep." The identification of Jesus as the great Shepherd of the sheep plays a big part in God being a God who makes his people whole, complete, and satisfied.

Have you ever thought about the implications of Jesus being the great Shepherd and how that makes us complete, whole, and satisfied before God? The shepherd imagery conveys not just care but absolute sovereignty over the sheep. The domestic sheep of the first century could not live without a shepherd. They will not find food or water on their own. They cannot fight predators. They cannot find their way back to the sheep pen. That is the sheep. That is us.

We are not the heroes of our own story. When you read your Bible, you will find that we are the weak, the helpless, the needy ones. We are never identified as the hero. We are the sheep. Peter says: "For you were like sheep going astray." And the God of peace did not leave us to ourselves. He gave us a shepherd. Peter concludes: "but have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls" — the Lord Jesus Christ, the great Shepherd who shed his blood for you.

Make no mistake: we did not return to him because we found him attractive. Isaiah 52 and 53 tells us he was very unattractive to the natural man. We came to him because he sought us out and brought us back to the fold. Your shepherd went after you and brought you into the fold through the blood of the everlasting covenant. Jesus himself says:

> *"I am the good shepherd; and I know My sheep, and am known by My own. As the Father knows Me, even so I know the Father; and I lay down My life for the sheep."* (John 10:14-15)

The God of peace creates peace in our hearts and lives as we trust our shepherd fully. The closer we are to Christ, the more we trust in him, the more peace we experience in our lives. Jesus is the great Shepherd — there is no one greater, none who can do a better job in bringing peace, wholeness, and satisfaction to us.

And our great Shepherd is a tender shepherd:

> *"He will feed His flock like a shepherd; He will gather the lambs with His arm, and carry them in His bosom, and gently lead those who are with young."* (Isaiah 40:11)

That is our shepherd. That is who God is: the God of peace who brought our Lord Jesus Christ from the dead, the great Shepherd of the sheep.

## II. What God Does: Equip Us for Every Good Work

This is all description of who God is. Now let us look at what God does in verse 21:

> *"Make you complete in every good work to do His will, working in you what is well pleasing in His sight through Jesus Christ."* (Hebrews 13:21)

That is what God does. God equips us. Keep in mind that this equipping is still in the context of God being the God of peace. The phrase "make you complete" means to put into proper condition, to equip, to restore, to mend. This same word is used in Matthew 4 to describe the disciples mending nets on the shore. If you have huge holes in your net you are not going to catch any fish. For the net to function as it was intended, it has to be mended. That is what this word means. It is also used in Galatians 6 for the restoration of a brother who fell into sin:

> *"Brethren, if a man is overtaken in any trespass, you who are spiritual restore such a one."* (Galatians 6:1)

In the extrabiblical writings of the first century, this was the word for setting a bone straight — when someone dislocated an arm or broke a leg, a physician would set it straight. That is the word for "making you complete in every good work."

So how does God equip us through the blood of the eternal covenant to be whole, satisfied, and at peace? In our translation we read "in every good work," but a better way to think of it is "*with* every good work" — he equips us *with* every good thing. There is absolutely nothing we need to be whole, complete, and satisfied that God does not provide through the blood of the eternal covenant. The blood of Jesus equips us completely to do what God designed us to do. We do not need something else.

### Running to the Wrong Things

We are often tempted to seek peace and wholeness somewhere else, just like the Hebrew Christians were. We run to substances to provide the peace and wholeness that only Christ provides. We run to relationships to provide the peace and wholeness that only Christ can provide. We run to arrogance — "if I think highly enough of myself, maybe I will feel whole." We run to our body image to provide the peace and wholeness that only Christ provides. We run to our sexuality to provide the peace and wholeness that only Christ provides.

And in the running, we leave behind every good thing that is entirely sufficient for our wholeness and satisfaction. Those things we run to may provide temporary relief — we may find some peace in them for a while. But eventually they will only destroy us.

People of God, your Shepherd equips you. Instead of running away from him, we need to run to him, into him, to be satisfied with him.

### The Means of Equipping

The God of peace equips us sometimes directly, but more often than not through means.

He equips us to be whole through the Word. In 2 Timothy 3:16-17, Paul says:

> *"All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work."* (2 Timothy 3:16-17)

That is the same word as in Hebrews 13. The Word of God, as we read it and hear it proclaimed, equips us to do everything that God calls us to do.

He equips us to be whole through his Spirit. In John 16, Jesus says he is going to send his Spirit, and the Spirit will lead us into all things.

He equips us to be whole through his shepherds. In Ephesians 4:11-13, Paul says that God gave pastor-teachers to the church to equip the saints for the work of the ministry.

He equips us to be whole through his people. Ephesians 4 goes on to say that as the people are equipped by the pastor, they then build each other up toward the completeness of their head, the Lord Jesus Christ.

God is equipping us, and God is at work in us through Jesus Christ. The Father works in us to will and to do what is pleasing to him. And when we run from Christ and try to find wholeness and peace somewhere else, we are also running from the work of God that makes us satisfied through obedience.

A lot of times people say, "I am feeling down. I do not feel whole. I do not feel at peace. I am just going to stay home today." Or: "I really feel down. I do not feel satisfied. I am not going to read my Bible. I am not going to pray." In doing those things, we are forsaking the very means that God has given us to help us be at peace and whole in him.

The time to be at church is all the time, but especially those times when you do not want to be at church. The time to read your Bible is all the time, but especially those times when you do not feel like doing it. The time to pray is all the time, especially those times when you do not feel like doing it.

Do you know who loves it when we are not in church, not praying, not reading the Bible? Satan does. He does not want you at peace. He does not want you to find your fulfillment in Christ. He would be happy if you keep on running away and finding fulfillment in all these other things that will distract you from Christ — things that will not give you any eternal peace.

## III. How We Respond: Obedience and Doxology

So how do we respond to who God is and what God does? Look at the end of verse 21. The author says that God is doing all these things "through Jesus Christ, to whom be glory forever and ever."

We do his will. He is equipping us in order for us to do his will. This cannot be lost on us. It is not for self-actualization. It is not to "be our best self." It is to do his will, to obey him, which is the same thing as obedience to his Word. We are at our best when we are obeying God.

You may have heard the saying, "Let go and let God" — it came from the Keswick movement in Great Britain. The idea is that you simply sit there and God acts in you automatically. That is not in the Bible. Perhaps if we changed it to "let go and *obey* God" — do whatever the Bible tells you — that would be more accurate. Because that is what we are being equipped to do. Paul marries these two ideas in Philippians 2:12-13: God is working in us as we work to do his will.

We also respond by glorifying Christ. As is often the case, the contemplation of the work of Christ causes the Christian to break forth in doxology. The author of Hebrews is blessing the people and reminding them of the work of Christ — and as he does that, he cannot help but to ascend in praise, in the doxology at the end of verse 21: "to whom be glory forever and ever."

The response to who God is and what he does for us — making us whole, complete, at peace — is making much of him. Words, deeds, thoughts, attitudes — everything bursting with the praises of Christ. That is how we respond to God: doing his will, glorifying Christ, making much of him in everything in our lives.

Peace, fullness, and satisfaction can be elusive, can be difficult to find — but only when we are running *from* the God of peace instead of *into* the God of peace.

May the God of peace who raised from the dead our great Shepherd, the Lord Jesus Christ, grant you peace. Grant you wholeness. Grant you satisfaction through the blood of the everlasting covenant that our risen Savior shed for you — to whom be glory forever and ever.
